recommendations: Use watchPosition (not repeated getCurrentPosition) with { enableHighAccuracy: true, maximumAge: 0, timeout: 10000 }; save the returned watch id and call clearWatch(id) in the React effect cleanup to avoid leaks and battery drain.

Treat speed and heading as optional: render them only when non-null and accuracy is acceptable. Provide a fallback that computes heading from consecutive lat/lon deltas (haversine bearing) for when the GPS heading is null at low speed.

On iOS, add a one-time 'Enable compass' button that calls DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission() on tap, then read webkitCompassHeading for a stationary heading; fall back to GeolocationCoordinates.heading while moving.

Proxy Overpass and Nominatim through Next.js Route Handlers (server-side fetch) to eliminate CORS, set a descriptive User-Agent (e.g. 'DashcamApp/1.0 (dudungdotnet@gmail.com)'), and centralize caching plus rate limiting.

Overpass query for road data: [out:json][timeout:25]; way(around:25,LAT,LON)[highway]; out tags geom; then post-process to pick the nearest way by point-to-segment distance and read tags.oneway and tags.maxspeed.
Handle oneway values fully: 'yes', 'no', '-1'/'reverse' (opposite to node order), 'reversible'; infer oneway=yes for motorways and junction=roundabout when the tag is absent.
Normalize maxspeed: parse plain km/h numbers, 'NN mph' (convert), 'none' (e.g. German autobahn), and implicit codes (maxspeed:type / zone tags); fall back to per-country defaults when missing.

Geohash/grid-snap each GPS fix (round coords to ~4-5 decimals or geohash length 7-8) and key the cache by that cell so the same road segment is fetched once; use Upstash/Vercel KV with a multi-hour-to-day TTL plus an in-memory LRU.

Strictly rate-limit Nominatim to <=1 request/second and debounce reverse-geocode calls by distance (only when moved >50-100m or the address cell changed); cache results and never use it for autocomplete or bulk/grid queries.
Always display OSM attribution '© OpenStreetMap contributors' (ODbL) wherever Overpass/Nominatim data is shown.
Implement HTTP 429/504 handling: exponential backoff, a server-side request queue, and a secondary Overpass mirror (overpass.kumi.systems) as failover.
If usage exceeds light/personal scale, self-host Overpass and/or Nominatim (or use a commercial provider) — the public OSM instances are not meant for production traffic from many users.
pitfalls: heading is null whenever speed is 0 and is unreliable at low speed; never assume it's always present. Same for speed on non-GPS/desktop positioning.
Without enableHighAccuracy:true, speed/heading are almost always null and accuracy is poor (Wi-Fi/IP based, 20-100m+), which is unusable for a dashcam.
Geolocation requires HTTPS — it silently fails on plain HTTP in production (localhost is the only exempt origin).

iOS Safari's GeolocationCoordinates.heading only updates while moving and the DeviceOrientation alpha is non-absolute; you must use webkitCompassHeading and request orientation permission via a user gesture (iOS 13+).
Calling Overpass/Nominatim directly from the browser usually fails with CORS errors on public instances — proxy through your server.
Nominatim blocks requests with no/invalid User-Agent and enforces 1 req/s; hammering it (or using it for autocomplete/per-tick reverse geocoding) gets your IP banned.
Overpass public instances return HTTP 429 (rate limited) and HTTP 504 (resource/timeout) under load; a naive retry loop makes it worse — use backoff.
Overpass has no distance/ORDER BY function, so 'nearest road' must be computed in your own code from the returned geometry; an around radius too small misses the road, too large returns many ways.
maxspeed is frequently missing or non-numeric ('none', 'NN mph', signed/implicit zone codes); a parser that assumes an integer km/h will break.
oneway is often implicit (motorways, roundabouts) and uses '-1' to mean 'one-way against the digitization direction' — mishandling sign flips the displayed travel direction.
Filesystem caching does not work on serverless (Vercel/Netlify) — use Vercel KV/Upstash Redis or another external store; in-memory caches are per-instance and reset on cold starts.
Vercel serverless functions have ~10s execution and ~4.5MB payload limits; keep Overpass timeouts and bounding boxes small or you'll hit them.
Without geohash/grid snapping, every GPS tick (often 1/sec) becomes a new Overpass+Nominatim call, instantly blowing the fair-use budget.
OSM data accuracy varies by region; rural roads may lack maxspeed/oneway entirely — design the UI to degrade gracefully when data is unknown.
libraries: No API key needed for any of these. Optional helpers: ngeohash (geohash grid keys for caching), @turf/turf or just a small haversine/bearing helper (nearest-segment distance + heading fallback), lru-cache (in-memory cache), @upstash/redis or @vercel/kv (persistent serverless cache), @upstash/ratelimit (Nominatim 1 req/s limiting), p-queue (server-side request queue/throttle), and overpass-frontend or query-overpass if you want a typed Overpass client (or just fetch). For maps/visualization, react-leaflet + leaflet with OSM tiles (also requires OSM attribution).

crypto.summary: The dashcam-project (Next.js 16.2.9, React 19, pnpm) already has a working server-side signing layer in lib/crypto/ (sign.ts, verify.ts, canonical.ts, keys.ts) and domain types in lib/evidence/types.ts. Installed and ready: @noble/ed25519@3.1.0, @noble/hashes@2.2.0, @react-pdf/renderer@4.5.1, qrcode@1.5.4, idb. NOT yet built: any app/api/* routes (no /api/seal, /api/public-key, PDF report route, or /verify page), the @react-pdf/renderer report component, and QR wiring. The architecture is sound and the existing crypto is largely v3-correct. FLOW: (1) client builds EvidencePayload with sha-256 MediaHashes of imagery (signature commits to media without bloating the envelope); (2) POST to a Node-runtime /api/seal that stamps the authoritative server sealedAt, canonicalizes, sha-256 hashes, Ed25519-signs with the env-var key, returns SealedEvidence; (3) a Node-runtime PDF route renders @react-pdf/renderer with a human-readable report, a signature block (algorithm, publicKeyId, payloadHash, signature, sealedAt) and a QR (qrcode.toDataURL PNG) linking to a public /verify page; (4) /verify + /api/public-key let anyone re-verify in-browser via verifySealed against the published key. VERIFIED: noble v3 prohibits hex-string inputs (Uint8Array only) and the existing sign/verify correctly convert via hexToBytes/utf8ToBytes; signAsync/verifyAsync default to hashes.sha512Async which falls back to WebCrypto crypto.subtle SHA-512, so NO ed.hashes.sha512 assignment is needed for the async path (only for the sync sign()/verify()). Runs unchanged on Node serverless and in the browser.
recommendations: Build app/api/seal/route.ts with `export const runtime = 'nodejs'` (NOT edge). IGNORE any client sealedAt and stamp it server-side (the design intent: 'server-stamped, authoritative'), then call sealPayload(). Whitelist/validate payload fields server-side before signing so clients cannot inject arbitrary JSON into a signed artifact.

Build the PDF route with `export const runtime = 'nodejs'`. In next.config.ts add serverExternalPackages: ['@react-pdf/renderer'] (Next 16 renamed serverComponentsExternalPackages -> serverExternalPackages) to stop the bundler mangling react-pdf — this is the documented fix for the 'PDFDocument/Component is not a constructor' error in App Router route handlers. Use renderToBuffer and return the Buffer with Content-Type application/pdf.

Define the PDF as a .tsx component using @react-pdf primitives (Document/Page/View/Text/Image). Generate the QR with `await QRCode.toDataURL(verifyUrl)` BEFORE renderToBuffer and pass the data URL into <Image src={dataUrl}/>; never rely on network fetches at render time.

Fonts: register LOCAL bundled TTF/OTF (not a Google Fonts WOFF2 URL). Serverless cold starts can run renderToBuffer before a remote font downloads (silent fallback), and react-pdf supports TTF/OTF far better than WOFF2. Built-in Helvetica is the zero-risk option.

Add /api/public-key returning getPublicKeyInfo() (publicKeyHex, keyId, algorithm, isDev) so verifiers never have to trust the dashAI UI. Surface isDev prominently; keys.ts ships a hardcoded DEV_KEY_HEX fallback that must never yield authoritative evidence.

Build the public /verify page as a client component that reconstructs SealedEvidence (URL fragment or fetched id), fetches the published key, and calls verifySealed(). Display the two independent checks (hashMatches AND signatureValid) separately, as VerificationResult already exposes, so tampered-payload vs wrong-key failures are distinguishable.

Add REPLAY/FRESHNESS protection beyond the signature: Ed25519 does not stop a valid envelope being copied/re-submitted. Keep server-stamped sealedAt + unique eventId in the signed payload; on any endpoint that ACCEPTS sealed evidence, persist seen eventIds + reject duplicates, and reject sealedAt outside an acceptable window. For higher assurance treat sealedAt as an RFC-3161-style trusted timestamp.

Key management: keep DASHAI_SIGNING_KEY (32-byte hex Ed25519 seed) in Vercel Project Env Vars, server-only, never NEXT_PUBLIC_*. Use separate keys per environment; rotate by publishing multiple public keys keyed by publicKeyId (keyId already embeds a key fingerprint, enabling rotation without invalidating old signatures). Env-var secrets can leak via logs/dumps — for a higher bar move to KMS/HSM, but env var is acceptable here if Vercel project access is tightly controlled.

Optionally upgrade canonicalization toward RFC 8785 (JCS) if cross-language verification is ever needed. Current canonicalize() uses JS Array.sort() (UTF-16 code-unit order) vs JCS's Unicode code-POINT order, and relies on JSON.stringify number formatting — byte-stable JS-to-JS (all that's needed today) but would diverge from a Python/Go JCS impl for non-BMP keys or unusual numbers. Document the canonical form as 'JS JSON.stringify with recursively code-unit-sorted keys', not strict JCS.
pitfalls: Edge runtime trap: do NOT let seal or PDF routes use the Edge runtime. @react-pdf/renderer needs Node APIs and you want process.env for the key server-side. Always set `export const runtime = 'nodejs'`.

react-pdf App Router 'not a constructor' bug: without serverExternalPackages, Next's bundler mangles react-pdf and renderToBuffer throws 'PDFDocument/Component is not a constructor'. The single most common react-pdf-on-Next-App-Router failure.

Signing the wrong bytes: hash and sign the SAME canonical string. Existing code does this (canon -> sha256 for payloadHash, signs utf8ToBytes(canon)). Keep canonicalize() the single source of truth for both sign and verify or verification silently breaks.

Treating unsigned report content as attested: anything outside EvidencePayload (UI text, presentation fields) carries NO evidentiary weight. The PDF must visually scope what the signature covers (payload + media hashes) vs decorative content.

Media binding gap: the signature commits to sha-256 MediaHashes, not the pixels. If images are distributed separately, a verifier MUST re-hash received images and compare to mediaHashes, else images can be swapped while the signature still 'verifies'. Make /verify re-hash presented media.

What the signature does NOT prove (state this in the report): NOT that the camera witnessed physical reality, that GPS/speed/plate-OCR are accurate, anyone's identity, or that timestamps reflect true wall-clock beyond the server clock. It ONLY proves this exact payload was sealed by this dashAI key and is unaltered since sealedAt. dashAI is tamper-EVIDENT, not tamper-proof, and not a notary of facts.

DEV key leakage to production: keys.ts falls back to a hardcoded public DEV_KEY_HEX if DASHAI_SIGNING_KEY is missing/malformed. Fail closed (throw) in production or hard-gate report generation when isDev is true, rather than silently signing with a known key.

Replay/duplication: a valid sealed envelope can be replayed or reattached to another context. Signature gives integrity+authenticity but NOT freshness/uniqueness; add eventId de-dup and sealedAt windowing at consumers.

Canonical JSON edge cases: undefined drops; NaN/Infinity are not JSON-representable; floating-point coords/speeds can carry engine-dependent precision. Keep payload numbers plain finite JSON; today only the server hashes, which sidesteps most issues — preserve that invariant.

Web Crypto Ed25519 availability: native crypto.subtle Ed25519 only became broadly cross-browser in 2025 (~79%+ users). For max reach on public /verify, keep @noble/ed25519 for verification (zero-dep, works on older browsers) rather than depending solely on Web Crypto Ed25519.
libraries: @noble/ed25519@3.1.0 — Ed25519 sign/verify (installed). v3: Uint8Array inputs only (no hex strings); async methods use WebCrypto SHA-512 by default, so no ed.hashes.sha512 setup needed for signAsync/verifyAsync. Runs on Node serverless and in browser.
@noble/hashes@2.2.0 — sha256 for canonical payload hashing and media hashes; bytesToHex/hexToBytes/utf8ToBytes (already used in lib/crypto).
@react-pdf/renderer@4.5.1 — server-side PDF via renderToBuffer in a Node-runtime route; requires serverExternalPackages in next.config and local TTF/OTF fonts.
qrcode@1.5.4 (+ @types/qrcode) — QRCode.toDataURL(url) -> PNG data URL embeddable in react-pdf <Image>; generate before renderToBuffer.
Web Crypto API (crypto.subtle, { name: 'Ed25519' }) — optional native browser verification path; broadly supported as of 2025 but keep @noble as fallback for older clients.
idb@8 — already present; suitable for client-side persistence of pending/sealed events.
Optional strict RFC 8785 (JCS) library — only if cross-language (non-JS) verifiers are required; current JS-only canonicalize() suffices for JS-to-JS verification.

sensors.summary:
Research on using the browser DeviceMotion/DeviceOrientation APIs to auto-trigger evidence capture on a vehicle collision or hard-braking event, for the "dashai" Next.js 16 / React 19 dashcam project. The project already has a working baseline at /Users/ghaisan/Documents/dashcam-project/lib/sensors/motion.ts that listens to `devicemotion`, computes net g-force magnitude (preferring `acceleration`, falling back to `accelerationIncludingGravity` minus 1g baseline), debounces, and classifies hard-brake (>=0.75g) vs impact (>=2.5g). The implementation is fundamentally sound; the main gaps are false-positive hardening (no GPS-speed gating, no axis isolation, no peak-window/multi-sample confirmation), a too-low impact threshold for a true "crash," reliance on the often-unavailable gravity-free `acceleration` field, and a permission flow that must be driven from a real user gesture over HTTPS. Concrete thresholds, the iOS 13+ permission flow, sampling-rate realities, debouncing strategy, and pitfalls are below.

KEY API FACTS (MDN + W3C orientation-event spec):
- `devicemotion` gives accelerationIncludingGravity (raw, includes ~9.81 m/s^2 gravity) and acceleration (gravity-compensated). acceleration is frequently null on Android/older devices and on iOS unless the gyroscope-fused value is available, so accelerationIncludingGravity is the reliable field. Axes: x=west->east, y=south->north, z=down->up; units m/s^2. Magnitude g = sqrt(x^2+y^2+z^2)/9.81; with gravity included a device at rest reads ~1g, so the "net" excess is |mag-1| (only a rough approximation while the phone tilts).
- DeviceMotionEvent.interval = hardware sampling interval in ms (constant). Real-world rates: iOS caps third-party access ~100 Hz; browsers typically deliver ~60-67 Hz and can drop to as low as 1 Hz; Android SENSOR_DELAY_UI yields ~20-60 Hz. Low Power Mode and background/hidden tabs throttle or stop events (events only fire when document visibility is "visible").
- Secure context (HTTPS) is REQUIRED. Spec mandates precision clamping (acceleration to 0.1 m/s^2, orientation to 0.1 deg) for anti-fingerprinting.
- DeviceOrientationEvent gives alpha (0-360 yaw), beta (-180..180 pitch), gamma (-90..90 roll); a rollover shows up as beta/gamma swinging toward +-90. Also needs iOS permission.

THRESHOLDS (synthesized from fleet telematics + crash-detection patents/papers):
- Normal city driving: peaks ~0.2-0.3g.
- Harsh/hard braking & harsh accel/turn (Samsara/GPS Insight defaults): ~0.4-0.5g typical alert threshold, up to ~0.6-0.8g for "hard." Project's 0.75g hard-brake value is reasonable-to-conservative.
- Crash/impact: production smartphone systems trigger crashes around 2.0g and up; patents cite 3G/4G/8G tiers; heavier vehicles use higher thresholds. The project's 2.5g impact threshold is on the LOW side for a confident "collision" claim and will catch severe potholes/curb hits/phone knocks — recommend ~4g for a high-confidence crash (matching the well-known "speed >15 mph AND accel >4G" rule), with 2.0-2.5g treated as a lower-confidence "possible impact" that still seals footage but is labeled accordingly.

iOS 13+ PERMISSION FLOW: DeviceMotionEvent.requestPermission()/DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission() are static methods that exist only on iOS Safari (and iOS Chrome/Firefox which use WebKit). They MUST be called synchronously inside a user-gesture handler (click/tap) over HTTPS, else NotAllowedError. They return a Promise resolving to 'granted' | 'denied'. Feature-detect with `typeof DeviceMotionEvent.requestPermission === 'function'`; on non-iOS just add the listener (no prompt). Permission is per-session-ish and must be re-requested if the listener was never attached. The project's requestMotionPermission() already does this correctly; just ensure the call site is a button tap and that orientation permission is requested too if rollover detection is added.
recommendations: Use accelerationIncludingGravity as the primary signal, not acceleration. event.acceleration is null on most Android and many iOS devices, so the current `ev.acceleration ?? ev.accelerationIncludingGravity` path silently degrades. Always read accelerationIncludingGravity and subtract a dynamically estimated gravity baseline (a low-pass/EMA filter of the magnitude, alpha~0.8) instead of a fixed 1g, so phone tilt while mounted doesn't bias the net g. Net g = |instantaneous magnitude - lowpass baseline| / 9.81.

Adopt tiered thresholds: hard-brake/harsh ~0.45-0.75g (keep 0.75g if you want only clearly hard braking), 'possible impact' ~2.0-2.5g (seal footage, low confidence), and 'crash' >=4.0g (high confidence). Reserve the strong wording/auto-report for the >=4g tier. Optionally distinguish hard braking from impact by axis: braking is dominated by the vehicle's longitudinal axis with a sustained ramp, whereas a collision is a sharp omnidirectional spike with high jerk (d(accel)/dt).

Gate on GPS speed to kill the dominant false positives. Adopt the well-established rule: only treat a high-g spike as a crash if GPS shows the vehicle was moving (>~15 mph / ~6.7 m/s) before the event AND/OR speed drops sharply right after. The project already collects GeoPoint with speedMps in lib/evidence/types.ts and lib/geo/location.ts, so cross-reference the latest fix. If accel spikes but GPS speed is unchanged, it's almost certainly a phone drop/knock, not a crash.

Confirm with a short peak-detection window rather than a single sample. Buffer the last ~1-2s of magnitude samples (a ring buffer). Fire only when the peak exceeds threshold AND enough samples corroborate (e.g. the event spans >1 sample but resolves within the window, ruling out a single spurious reading). Report the true peakG over the window, not the first sample that crossed. This also lets you compute jerk to separate crashes from bumps.

Keep debouncing but split it from cooldown. The 3000ms debounce prevents duplicate fires from one impact — keep it. Separately, after a confirmed capture, apply a longer cooldown (e.g. 10-30s) before re-arming auto-capture so a multi-impact collision or post-crash jostling doesn't spawn many evidence bundles. Always report the max peak within the debounce window.

Drive the permission request from an explicit user gesture over HTTPS and surface state in the UI. requestMotionPermission() is correct, but it must be invoked from an onClick/onTouch handler (e.g. an 'Enable crash detection' button), not on mount/useEffect, or iOS throws NotAllowedError. Show granted/denied state, and on non-iOS attach the listener directly. Re-request on each new session since the grant is not durable.

Handle the lifecycle and throttling realities: events stop when the tab is hidden or backgrounded and slow dramatically in iOS Low Power Mode. For a dashcam the screen should be kept awake (Screen Wake Lock API) and the tab foregrounded; warn the user that locking the phone or Low Power Mode disables detection. Re-attach/verify the listener on visibilitychange.

Add DeviceOrientationEvent-based rollover detection as a complementary, higher-severity signal: a sustained beta or gamma swing toward +-90 degrees (vehicle on its side/roof) over a short window. It needs its own iOS requestPermission() call (request both in the same gesture). Treat rollover as top-severity, bypassing the speed gate.

On a confirmed event, auto-trigger the evidence pipeline: seal the surrounding video buffer plus a MotionEvent record (peakG, kind, axis breakdown, timestamp) and the concurrent GeoPoint, then run it through the existing seal flow. Consider extending EvidencePayload (lib/evidence/types.ts) or ViolationEvent with a motion/impact field and adding a 'kecelakaan' (accident) ReportKind path so crash captures are cryptographically sealed like violation evidence.

Calibrate empirically and make thresholds configurable. Log raw magnitude traces during real test drives (normal, hard brake, speed bumps, curb strikes, phone-on-seat) to tune per-device, since sampling rate and noise floor vary widely (60-100 Hz, precision clamped to 0.1 m/s^2). Expose thresholds as constants/settings rather than hard-coding, given the wide device variance.
pitfalls: event.acceleration (gravity-free) is null on most Android and many iOS devices; code that prefers it silently falls back and the fixed '-1g' baseline is wrong whenever the mounted phone is tilted, biasing net g. Use accelerationIncludingGravity with a dynamic low-pass gravity estimate.

A single high-g sample is not a crash. Phone drops, knocks against the mount, dropping the phone on the seat, slamming a door, potholes, speed bumps, and curb strikes all produce 2-4g+ spikes. Without GPS-speed gating and peak-window/jerk confirmation, the current 2.5g impact threshold will false-positive frequently.

iOS requestPermission() throws NotAllowedError if not called synchronously inside a user gesture, and the whole API is blocked on non-HTTPS origins. Calling it from useEffect/onload fails. requestPermission exists ONLY on iOS WebKit — feature-detect or you crash on Android/desktop.

devicemotion events stop when the tab/app is hidden or the screen locks, and slow to as low as 1 Hz in iOS Low Power Mode — exactly the conditions during/after a real crash when the driver can't touch the phone. Detection silently dies unless you hold a Wake Lock and keep the page foregrounded; even then a locked phone defeats it.

Sampling rate is non-deterministic and capped (~60-100 Hz, sometimes far lower) and the spec clamps acceleration precision to 0.1 m/s^2 for anti-fingerprinting, so very brief impact peaks can be under-sampled or rounded. Don't assume you'll catch the true peak; widen the detection window.

Magnitude-only detection cannot distinguish a hard brake (sustained longitudinal ramp) from a collision (sharp omnidirectional spike) or from device handling — the project's kind classification by magnitude alone will mislabel events. Use axis decomposition and jerk.

Orientation/motion permission is per-session and not durable; if the grant lapses or the listener was never attached, detection appears 'on' in the UI but receives nothing. Verify the listener is actually firing.

The 2.5g impact threshold is too low to assert a 'collision' for evidentiary/legal purposes (this project produces signed legal evidence). Overclaiming a crash from a pothole undermines the evidence's credibility; gate strong claims behind ~4g + speed + multi-sensor corroboration and label lower-confidence tiers honestly.

Mount orientation/looseness changes the axis mapping and adds vibration noise; a phone vibrating in a loose mount raises the noise floor and can mask or mimic events. Per-device/per-mount calibration is needed.
libraries: Web DeviceMotionEvent API (window 'devicemotion' event; accelerationIncludingGravity, acceleration, rotationRate, interval) — primary signal, already used in lib/sensors/motion.ts
Web DeviceOrientationEvent API (window 'deviceorientation' event; alpha/beta/gamma) — for rollover detection
Geolocation API (already wrapped in lib/geo/location.ts; GeoPoint.speedMps in lib/evidence/types.ts) — for GPS speed gating to cut false positives
Screen Wake Lock API (navigator.wakeLock) — keep screen awake so motion events keep firing during dashcam use
Generic Sensor API Accelerometer/LinearAccelerationSensor (optional, Chromium-only) — higher-fidelity alternative to DeviceMotion where supported, with explicit frequency control
No third-party npm dependency needed; existing project deps (zustand for state, idb for buffering) suffice. Avoid pulling in a library for this.

context.summary:
Research synthesis for a citizen dashcam traffic-enforcement app in Indonesia, covering (1) the ETLE system, (2) admissibility of citizen-submitted video, and (3) obligations under UU No. 27/2022 (PDP), with concrete privacy-by-design implications.

1) ETLE SYSTEM (state-run): Electronic Traffic Law Enforcement is the Polri/Korlantas system that automatically captures violations via fixed CCTV plus a growing "ETLE Mobile" fleet (dashcam, helmet-cam, body-cam, and drone-cam on patrol vehicles), increasingly using AI to detect ~12 violation types (red-light, no-helmet, no-seatbelt, phone use, speeding, odd-even, overload, etc.). Workflow: camera captures evidence with time/location/type -> data goes to a Back Office -> a human officer verifies and matches the plate against the Regident/vehicle database -> a confirmation notice (surat konfirmasi) is sent to the registered owner -> if confirmed/unpaid, payment via virtual account and STNK (registration) can be blocked. Korlantas is centralizing ETLE nationally (target ~5,000 cameras by 2027) and has rolled out ETLE Handheld devices that print tickets on site. Key design lesson: ETLE is two-stage — automated DETECTION followed by mandatory HUMAN VERIFICATION and an owner-confirmation step before any sanction. No fully-automated punishment.

2) CITIZEN VIDEO ADMISSIBILITY: There is NO established legal mechanism for citizens to submit private dashcam footage as direct evidence for a tilang. Police (Dirlantas Polda Metro Jaya) have explicitly stated private-car dashcam recordings "belum bisa" be used as evidence because the regulation and SOP do not yet exist. Only recordings from police/official-duty vehicles feed the official ETLE evidentiary chain. BUT: (a) Electronically, UU ITE (Pasal 5 ayat (1)-(2)) makes Electronic Information/Documents and their printouts valid evidence in addition to KUHAP Pasal 184, provided integrity/authenticity is preserved and the recording was lawfully obtained; CCTV/video is routinely accepted in court as alat bukti (esp. as "petunjuk"). (b) In practice, citizen/viral footage and reports filed via Polri's public-complaint channels (Dumas Presisi / PRESISI super-app, WhatsApp 0855-5555-4141) can TRIGGER police follow-up/investigation, but they are leads, not self-executing tickets — an officer must still verify and act. So an app should be framed as a reporting/lead-generation tool feeding human police review, NOT as an evidence-grade enforcement system that issues penalties itself.

3) UU PDP No. 27/2022 OBLIGATIONS: Recording strangers' faces and plates is "pemrosesan data pribadi." A face is "data pribadi spesifik/biometrik" (Pasal 4) requiring stricter protection and explicit consent; name/address/license plate are general personal data (data is still personal because a plate is linkable to an identifiable owner). The app operator is a Pengendali Data Pribadi. Pasal 20 sets the lawful bases (explicit consent; contract; legal obligation; vital interest; public-task/public interest; legitimate interest). Crucially, the public-interest and law-enforcement EXCEPTIONS in Pasal 15 (penegakan hukum, keamanan nasional, kepentingan umum penyelenggaraan negara, etc.) are available to STATE actors carrying out those functions — a private app cannot simply self-declare itself exempt; it generally cannot obtain consent from filmed bystanders, so it must rely carefully on legitimate-interest balancing and data minimization, and route enforcement-purpose data to the competent authority. Core controller duties: lawful basis + transparency/notice (Pasal 20-21), purpose limitation and data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation with deletion/destruction after retention period or on request (Pasal 16), security safeguards, DPO where required, and breach notification in writing within 3x24 hours to data subjects and the authority (Pasal 46). Data subject rights (Pasal 5-13) include access, correction, erasure, objection, and withdrawal of consent. Sanctions range from administrative fines to criminal liability for unlawfully obtaining/disclosing others' personal data; publicly posting identifiable footage without basis can itself violate PDP (and risk defamation/UU ITE exposure).
recommendations: Position the product as a REPORTING/LEAD tool that feeds human police review, not a self-executing enforcement system. Mirror ETLE's two-stage model: automated capture/detection, then mandatory human (police) verification before any consequence. Never let the app assert guilt or issue penalties.

Apply privacy-by-design distinction DETECTION vs RECOGNITION: do on-device DETECTION only (a face/plate is present, a violation is occurring) and avoid biometric face RECOGNITION/matching against any identity database. Face recognition triggers Pasal 4 'data biometrik/spesifik' with explicit-consent requirements you cannot realistically obtain from filmed bystanders.

Blur by default at capture: auto-blur all faces and (for anyone other than the reported violator) license plates before storage/upload and before any human reviewer sees the clip. Keep the reported violator's plate unblurred only in the package that is transmitted to police, not in any user-visible or public surface.

Do NOT publish, share, or socially expose submitted footage. Public posting of identifiable faces/plates without a lawful basis can violate UU PDP (and risk UU ITE defamation exposure). Route evidence privately to the competent authority (Korlantas/Polda) or via official channels like Dumas Presisi rather than feeds.

Establish a lawful basis and explicit notice: the operator is a Pengendali Data Pribadi under Pasal 20. Since bystander consent is impractical, rely on a documented legitimate-interest / public-interest assessment (DPIA-style balancing test) AND get explicit consent from the APP USER (the recorder) for their own footage processing; provide a clear privacy notice covering purpose, data types, retention, recipients, and rights.

Implement strict data minimization and retention limits (Pasal 16): collect only the clip segment and metadata needed for the specific report; auto-delete after a short, defined retention window or after the report is closed/rejected; honor erasure and consent-withdrawal requests. Default to deletion of non-violation footage immediately on-device.

Build PDP compliance machinery: appoint/assign a DPO function, maintain a record of processing, implement encryption and access controls, log access, and stand up a breach-response process that notifies affected data subjects and the data protection authority in writing within 3x24 hours (Pasal 46).

Engage Korlantas/Polda formally before launch. There is currently NO SOP for citizen dashcam evidence; pursue an MoU or integration with the official ETLE / Dumas Presisi pipeline so submissions become legitimate police leads, the police (a Pasal 15 law-enforcement actor) handle the evidentiary/identity-matching step, and the app stays on the right side of the consent gap.

Preserve evidentiary integrity for clips routed to police: keep original (pre-blur) source secured with hashing/chain-of-custody, capture reliable timestamp/geolocation metadata, and avoid edits — UU ITE/KUHAP admissibility depends on authenticity and that the recording was lawfully obtained.

Add anti-abuse and anti-doxxing controls: prevent users from downloading/re-sharing others' footage, prohibit bounty/vigilante framing, require user identity verification for submissions (as Dumas Presisi does), and screen out clips that primarily expose innocent bystanders.
pitfalls: Assuming citizen dashcam footage can directly trigger a tilang: police have stated private-vehicle dashcam recordings are NOT yet admissible evidence because no regulation/SOP exists; only police-vehicle ETLE recordings feed the official chain.

Assuming the Pasal 15 PDP exceptions (law enforcement / public interest) cover a private app: those exceptions are for state actors performing those functions; a private operator cannot self-declare exemption and must justify processing under Pasal 20 (legitimate interest) with safeguards.

Treating faces and plates as low-risk data: faces are 'data pribadi spesifik/biometrik' (Pasal 4) needing the strictest protection; plates are personal data because they link to an identifiable owner. Both are in scope of UU PDP.

Doing face RECOGNITION/identity matching client- or server-side: this escalates to high-risk biometric processing requiring explicit consent (impossible to get from bystanders) and a DPIA; detection-only avoids this.
Publishing or letting users share submitted clips (naming-and-shaming): high risk of UU PDP violation plus UU ITE defamation exposure and vigilante/doxxing harm.
No retention/deletion policy: storing footage indefinitely breaches Pasal 16 storage limitation; non-violation footage especially must not be retained.
Ignoring breach-notification timing: failure to notify subjects and the authority within 3x24 hours (Pasal 46) is itself a violation.
Over-editing or losing chain-of-custody on evidence sent to police, undermining UU ITE/KUHAP authenticity requirements and making the report useless and potentially challengeable.

Building a bounty/reward model without legal grounding: there is no confirmed national reward program for citizen traffic reports, and incentivized mass-surveillance of strangers amplifies PDP and abuse risks.

doc_standards.summary: Research complete on canonical structures for 15 software-engineering document types plus professional formatting conventions, drawn from primary standards (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148, 42010, 29119-3, 26514, IEEE 829), the C4 model (c4model.com), Michael Nygard's ADR + MADR, OWASP threat-modeling guidance, the OpenAPI 3.1 spec, GDPR Article 35 / ICO DPIA guidance, and established Vision/Scope (Karl Wiegers), PRD, runbook, RTM, risk-register, and RACI practice. Each document's canonical section list is given below in the recommendations array (one entry per document = its required sections), with cross-cutting pitfalls, the standards/libraries to anchor on, and sources. NOTE on the target repo: /Users/ghaisan/Documents/dashcam-project is a Next.js 15 (App Router) + TypeScript + pnpm app (see /Users/ghaisan/Documents/dashcam-project/package.json, /app, /lib) with essentially empty CLAUDE.md and a stub README.md — no docs/ folder exists yet, so these documents would be authored from scratch (suggest a top-level docs/ directory). This was a research task; no files were created or modified.
recommendations: VISION & SCOPE (Karl Wiegers template). Canonical sections: 1. Business Requirements (1.1 Background; 1.2 Business Opportunity; 1.3 Business Objectives; 1.4 Success Metrics; 1.5 Vision Statement; 1.6 Business Risks; 1.7 Business Assumptions and Dependencies). 2. Scope and Limitations (2.1 Major Features; 2.2 Scope of Initial Release; 2.3 Scope of Subsequent Releases; 2.4 Limitations and Exclusions). 3. Business Context (3.1 Stakeholder Profiles; 3.2 Project Priorities — using the scope/schedule/cost/quality/staff constraint-driver-DOF model; 3.3 Deployment Considerations). Answers 'why are we doing this'; sits above the PRD/SRS.

PRD (Product Requirements Document). Canonical sections: Document header/control (title, author, status, reviewers, last-updated); Overview/Summary; Problem statement & background; Goals and non-goals (explicit out-of-scope); Success metrics / KPIs & release criteria; Personas / target users & market assessment; Assumptions, constraints & dependencies; User stories / use cases / user flows; Functional requirements (prioritized, e.g. MoSCoW); UX / design (wireframes, mockups); Non-functional / system & technical requirements (performance, security, scalability, compliance); Release plan / milestones / timeline & phasing; Open questions; Appendix/references. PRD is product-facing (the 'what/why for the market'); the SRS is the engineering-precise descendant.

SRS — ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 (supersedes IEEE 830). Canonical SRS outline: 1. Introduction (1.1 Purpose; 1.2 Scope; 1.3 Product perspective; 1.4 Product functions; 1.5 User characteristics; 1.6 Limitations/constraints; 1.7 Definitions, acronyms & abbreviations). 2. References. 3. Specific requirements (3.1 External interfaces — user/hardware/software/communications; 3.2 Functional requirements [each: condition, subject, action, object, constraint of action]; 3.3 Usability/quality-in-use; 3.4 Performance; 3.5 Logical database/data requirements; 3.6 Design constraints; 3.7 System software quality attributes — reliability, availability, security, maintainability, portability; 3.8 Other / compliance). 4. Verification (mirrors §3 — how each requirement is verified). 5. Appendices (incl. assumptions/dependencies; acronyms). NOTE: 29148 also defines two sibling docs — StRS (Stakeholder Requirements Spec) and SyRS (System Requirements Spec). Every requirement must be uniquely identified, verifiable, unambiguous, traceable.

SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT — ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010 + C4. From 42010, a conforming Architecture Description must contain: Architecture identification/overview & version control; Stakeholders; Concerns (mapped to stakeholders); Architecture Viewpoints (each names the concerns it frames + its model kinds); Architecture Views (one per viewpoint, composed of architecture models); Model kinds; Architecture relations/correspondences and correspondence rules (consistency across views); Architecture Decisions and Rationale; Known inconsistencies. Layer the C4 model views inside: Level 1 System Context diagram; Level 2 Container diagram; Level 3 Component diagram; Level 4 Code (optional, often auto-generated); plus supplementary System Landscape, Dynamic, and Deployment diagrams. C4 is notation/tool independent and hierarchical (zoom-in). Common practical scaffold (arc42 or 4+1) can host both. Reference ADRs from the Decisions section.

ADR (Architecture Decision Record) — Michael Nygard format (the most-used; ~723 repos). Canonical sections (verbatim): Title (short noun phrase, numbered, e.g. ADR-0007); Status (proposed | accepted | rejected | deprecated | superseded by ADR-X); Context (the forces/issue motivating the decision); Decision (what we will do, active voice 'We will…'); Consequences (what becomes easier/harder — positive, negative, neutral). MADR (Markdown Any Decision Records) is the richer alternative adding: Context and Problem Statement; Decision Drivers; Considered Options; Decision Outcome (chosen option + justification); Pros/Cons of each option; Validation; More Information. Store as immutable, append-only, sequentially numbered files (docs/adr/).

DATA MODEL / ERD. Canonical sections: Introduction/purpose & scope; Modeling notation/conventions (Crow's Foot or Chen) and the three model levels — Conceptual ERD (entities + relationships, no attributes), Logical ERD (entities, attributes, keys, normalized, DBMS-independent), Physical ERD (tables, columns, datatypes, PK/FK, indexes, constraints); Entity catalog; Attributes (name, type, nullability, default, domain/range); Relationships with cardinality & optionality (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and referential-integrity rules; Primary/foreign/candidate keys; Data Dictionary (per-attribute definitions, sources, ownership); Normalization level & any deliberate denormalization with rationale; Reference/lookup data; Data lineage, retention & classification (esp. PII — ties to the DPIA); Assumptions/constraints; Diagram(s). Version-control the schema.

OPENAPI SPECIFICATION (v3.1.x — JSON/YAML, machine-readable). Canonical top-level objects: openapi (version string, REQUIRED); info (title + version REQUIRED; plus description, termsOfService, contact, license); servers (base URLs + variables); paths (endpoints → operations: parameters, requestBody, responses, security, tags, operationId, summary/description); webhooks (3.1+); components (REUSABLE: schemas, responses, parameters, examples, requestBodies, headers, securitySchemes, links, callbacks, pathItems); security (global requirements); tags (grouping + descriptions); externalDocs. In 3.1 at least one of paths, components, or webhooks must be present. Schemas use full JSON Schema 2020-12. Best practice: define error schemas, auth (securitySchemes), examples, and consistent naming; lint with Spectral; keep it the single source of truth for the API.

TEST PLAN — ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-3 (supersedes IEEE 829). Canonical Test Plan content items: 1. Overview/Introduction (1.1 Document identifier; 1.2 Scope; 1.3 References; 1.4 Glossary). 2. Context of testing (2.1 Project/test sub-process; 2.2 Test item(s); 2.3 Test scope — in/out; 2.4 Assumptions & constraints; 2.5 Stakeholders). 3. Testing communication. 4. Risk register (product risks + project risks). 5. Test strategy (5.1 Sub-processes; 5.2 Test deliverables; 5.3 Test design techniques; 5.4 Test completion/exit criteria; 5.5 Metrics; 5.6 Test data & environment requirements; 5.7 Retesting & regression; 5.8 Suspension/resumption criteria; 5.9 Deviations from the Organizational Test Strategy). 6. Testing activities and estimates. 7. Staffing (roles, training). 8. Schedule. The 29119-3 family also defines Test Status Report, Test Completion Report, Test Design/Case/Procedure Specifications, Test Data & Environment Requirements, Test Execution Log, and Test Incident (Defect/Anomaly) Report. (Legacy IEEE 829 'IEEE 829 Master/Level Test Plan' headings: Test plan identifier; Introduction; Test items; Features to be/not to be tested; Approach; Item pass/fail criteria; Suspension/resumption; Test deliverables; Testing tasks; Environmental needs; Responsibilities; Staffing & training; Schedule; Risks & contingencies; Approvals.)

SECURITY THREAT MODEL (STRIDE). Canonical sections: 1. Scope & objectives (system/feature analyzed, goals, date, participants, assumptions). 2. System decomposition / Architecture overview (Data Flow Diagrams — external entities, processes, data stores, data flows — with Trust Boundaries; assets; entry/exit points; privilege levels). 3. Threat identification per STRIDE category (Spoofing→Authentication, Tampering→Integrity, Repudiation→Non-repudiation, Information Disclosure→Confidentiality, Denial of Service→Availability, Elevation of Privilege→Authorization) — typically element-by-element. 4. Threat catalog/register (ID, STRIDE category, description, affected component/DFD element, risk rating — e.g. DREAD or CVSS/likelihood×impact, status). 5. Mitigations / countermeasures (per threat: Mitigate / Eliminate / Transfer / Accept(Avoid), owner, target date, residual risk). 6. Validation / assumptions / out-of-scope. 7. Review cadence. Anchor to OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet and tools like the Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool / OWASP Threat Dragon.

DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) — GDPR Art. 35 + ICO 7-step template. Canonical sections: 1. Identify the need for a DPIA (what the project is, why a DPIA is required/the screening trigger). 2. Describe the processing (systematic description: nature, scope, context, purposes; data flows; data categories incl. special-category data; volumes; retention; recipients; processors; international transfers). 3. Consultation process (data subjects, DPO, processors, other stakeholders). 4. Assess necessity and proportionality (lawful basis, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, retention, data-subject rights, processor compliance, transfer safeguards). 5. Identify and assess risks (source, likelihood and severity of risk to rights & freedoms → overall risk level). 6. Identify measures to reduce/eliminate risk (each measure: effect on risk, residual risk, measure approved). 7. Sign-off and record outcomes (DPO advice, residual-risk acceptance/owner, whether prior consultation with the supervisory authority is needed, review date). Must be done before high-risk processing begins; keep it a living document.

DEPLOYMENT RUNBOOK. Canonical sections: 1. Metadata header (service/system, version, owner/on-call, last-updated, environment, severity/criticality). 2. Overview & purpose. 3. Prerequisites (access/permissions, tools, approvals/change ticket, maintenance window, dependency status). 4. Pre-deployment checks (backups, DB migration readiness, feature flags, CI green, artifact/version to deploy). 5. Deployment procedure (numbered, copy-pasteable, sequential steps with expected output and a verification at each step). 6. Post-deployment validation (smoke tests, health checks, key metrics, observe ~15 min for stability). 7. Rollback procedure (trigger conditions/decision criteria, exact revert steps, how to confirm rollback succeeded). 8. Monitoring & alerts (dashboards, log locations, SLOs). 9. Communication plan (stakeholders, status updates, comms channels). 10. Troubleshooting / known issues / FAQ. 11. Contacts & escalation + related docs. Treat as version-controlled living document.

USER MANUAL — ISO/IEC/IEEE 26514 (design & development of information for users). Canonical sections: Title/identification & document control; Introduction (purpose, intended audience, scope, conventions/typographic, safety notices); Getting started (system requirements, installation, setup/configuration, first launch); Conceptual information (how it works, key concepts/architecture for users); Task/instructional information (step-by-step procedures, organized by user goal); Reference information (menus, commands, fields, options, settings, API/CLI reference); Troubleshooting & error messages (symptom→cause→resolution); FAQ; Glossary; Index; Appendices (shortcuts, accessibility, support/contact, licensing). 26514 emphasizes audience analysis, modularity, and the information models conceptual/instructional/reference/troubleshooting. Keep task-oriented, consistent, accessible.

PROJECT / SDLC PLAN with RACI. Canonical sections (aligns with PMBOK/IEEE SDLC): 1. Introduction (purpose, scope, references, definitions). 2. Project overview & objectives (deliverables, success criteria). 3. SDLC model & methodology (e.g. Agile/Scrum, Waterfall, phases: Plan→Requirements→Design→Develop→Test→Deploy→Operate/Maintain). 4. Organization & roles (org chart, stakeholders). 5. RACI matrix (rows = tasks/deliverables/decisions, columns = roles; cells = Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed — rule: ≥1 R per task and exactly 1 A per task). 6. Work breakdown structure (WBS) & deliverables. 7. Schedule & milestones (Gantt, dependencies, critical path). 8. Resource & budget/cost management. 9. Risk management approach (links to Risk Register). 10. Communication management plan. 11. Quality management & metrics. 12. Configuration/change management. 13. Procurement (if any). 14. Assumptions, constraints & dependencies. 15. Approval/sign-off.

RISK REGISTER. Canonical columns/fields (one row per risk): Risk ID; Date raised/identified; Risk category (technical, schedule, cost, security, compliance, operational, external); Risk description (cause → event → effect / 'if-then'); Probability/Likelihood (scale); Impact/Severity (scale); Risk score/level/rating (Likelihood × Impact, often RAG-coded); Risk owner; Trigger/early-warning indicators; Response strategy (Avoid / Mitigate / Transfer / Accept); Mitigation/contingency actions; Action owner & due date; Residual risk (post-mitigation likelihood/impact); Status (open/in-progress/closed/realized); Last reviewed / next review date; Notes/linked issues. Pair with a probability-impact matrix (heat map). Keep continuously updated; link to the Project Plan and Threat Model.

REQUIREMENTS TRACEABILITY MATRIX (RTM). Canonical columns (one row per requirement, providing bidirectional traceability): Requirement ID; Requirement description; Requirement type/category (functional/NFR); Source/origin (business need, stakeholder, PRD/Vision §, regulation); Priority; Owner; Design element / architecture component (forward trace); Code module/build (forward trace); Test case ID(s) & verification method (forward trace to Test Plan); Test/verification status (pass/fail/not-run); Defect/issue IDs; Implementation status; Comments/notes. Provides forward (need→requirement→design→code→test) and backward (test→requirement→source) traceability and coverage analysis (orphan requirements = untested; orphan tests = scope creep). Often a spreadsheet (xlsx) or generated by a requirements tool.

PROFESSIONAL DOCUMENT FORMATTING CONVENTIONS (apply to every document above). 1. Title page (document title, project/system name, doc type & ID, version/status, author(s), owner, classification, date, organization/logo). 2. Document control / version history table (version, date, author, description of change, reviewer/approver) + distribution list + sign-off/approval block. 3. Table of contents (auto-generated, with list of figures and list of tables) — plus page numbering in footer (commonly bottom of page; main-body numbering may start after front matter). 4. Numbered hierarchical headings (1, 1.1, 1.1.1) with consistent heading styles. 5. Figure and table captions (numbered, e.g. 'Figure 3-2: …', 'Table 4-1: …', with in-text cross-references and a caption convention). 6. Headers/footers (document title, version, confidentiality/classification marking, page X of Y). 7. Body text conventions: consistent professional font (~11–12pt, e.g. Calibri/Cambria/Times), consistent line spacing, justified or left-aligned body (be consistent), styled lists; callouts for notes/warnings. 8. Glossary + list of acronyms/abbreviations; References/bibliography; Appendices. 9. Consistency throughout (styles/templates), traceable cross-references, and version control. These signal production-grade quality and aid scanability.
libraries: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 — Requirements engineering (SRS/SyRS/StRS)
ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010:2022 (and :2011) — Architecture description
C4 model (c4model.com) — Simon Brown: Context/Container/Component/Code + Landscape/Dynamic/Deployment
arc42 — practical architecture-doc template (hosts 42010 + C4)
Michael Nygard ADR template; MADR (Markdown Any Decision Records); adr.github.io
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-3:2021 — Test documentation (supersedes IEEE 829-2008)
OpenAPI Specification v3.1.x (spec.openapis.org / OAI GitHub); Spectral for linting
OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet; Microsoft STRIDE; OWASP Threat Dragon / MS Threat Modeling Tool
GDPR Article 35 + ICO DPIA guidance/template; EDPB DPIA template
ISO/IEC/IEEE 26514:2022 — Design & development of information for users (user docs)
Karl Wiegers Vision & Scope template (Software Requirements, 3rd ed.)
PMBOK / RACI (responsibility assignment matrix); SDLC phase models
Crow's Foot & Chen ERD notation; conceptual/logical/physical data models
pitfalls: Conflating PRD, SRS, and Vision/Scope — they are distinct layers: Vision/Scope = why & boundaries (business), PRD = what for the market (product), SRS = engineering-precise, verifiable requirements. Authoring one and labeling it another is a common production-grade failure.

Citing IEEE 830 as current — it was superseded by ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 (2011/2018); 29148 adds the Verification section and the [condition][subject][action][object][constraint] functional-requirement syntax and requires every requirement be uniquely identified, verifiable, and traceable.

Citing IEEE 829 as current for test docs — superseded by ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-3, which uses flexible content items rather than a fixed document set and adds a risk register and test-completion reporting.

C4 is NOT defined inside ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010 — 42010 supplies the conceptual framework (stakeholders, concerns, viewpoints, views, decisions, correspondences); C4 supplies pragmatic views. A conforming Architecture Document must still cover the 42010 required elements (esp. decisions/rationale and view correspondences), not just draw C4 diagrams.

OpenAPI 3.1 vs 3.0 differences: 3.1 aligns with full JSON Schema 2020-12, adds webhooks, and only requires one of paths/components/webhooks — don't assume paths is mandatory or that 3.0 schema rules apply.

DPIA must be done BEFORE high-risk processing starts, must assess risks to data subjects' rights & freedoms (not just security), and requires documented DPO advice and sign-off with residual-risk owner; a security-only writeup is not a valid DPIA.
ADRs must be immutable and append-only — supersede with a new ADR and set Status: superseded; editing accepted ADRs destroys the decision history. Use sequential numbering.
STRIDE threat models are incomplete without explicit trust boundaries on the DFD and per-threat mitigation status with owners; a threat list with no mitigations/residual risk is not actionable.
RACI integrity rules: every task needs ≥1 Responsible and exactly one Accountable; multiple A's or zero A's is the classic RACI defect.
RTM must be bidirectional (forward and backward) — a one-directional matrix misses orphan requirements (untested) and orphan tests (scope creep / gold-plating).

Standards are paywalled (ISO/IEEE) — the exact normative clause text must come from the purchased standard; the section outlines here reflect the standards' published structure and reputable templates, which is sufficient to author conforming documents but verify clause numbering against the licensed copy.

style_guide.style_guide:
DASHAI DOCUMENTATION STYLE GUIDE (v1.0)
Production-grade, black-and-white, formal engineering documents. Authored from scratch into a new top-level docs/ directory of the dashAI repo (/Users/ghaisan/Documents/dashcam-project). All documents conform to the conventions below unless a per-document outline states otherwise.

1. SCOPE & GROUNDING
- Project: dashAI — a client-side (browser, Next.js 16 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript, pnpm) AI dashcam that runs on-device computer vision (TensorFlow.js / COCO-SSD, MediaPipe, Tesseract OCR) fused with GPS + OpenStreetMap (Overpass) to detect Indonesian traffic violations (UU 22/2009 LLAJ), then cryptographically seals evidence (Ed25519 over canonical JSON; sha-256 media hashes) into tamper-EVIDENT (not tamper-proof) verifiable reports.
- Authoritative terminology must match the codebase: "evidence payload" / EvidencePayload (the cryptographic boundary), "seal/sealed" (not "save"), "tamper-evident, not tamper-proof", "subject = self | other", "tier = core | secondary | cabin", "citation confidence = high | medium | low | pending", "ego-speed (GPS, accurate)" vs "other-speed (estimated)". Do not contradict lib/evidence/types.ts, lib/crypto/*, lib/legal/*.
- Bilingual rule: prose is English; Indonesian legal terms, statute text (bunyi pasal), violation labels (labelId), and sanctions are quoted verbatim in Bahasa Indonesia, with an English gloss in parentheses on first use. Statute citations follow "UU No. 22 Tahun 2009, Pasal NNN ayat (n)".

2. FILE FORMAT, OUTPUT & TOOLING
- Two authoring formats only: .docx for human-narrative, sign-off-bearing documents; .pdf (or machine-source) for fixed-layout, register, or machine-readable artifacts. The format per document is fixed in doc_outlines.
- Source-of-truth lives in docs/ under version control. Narrative docs are authored as .docx; the OpenAPI spec is authored as YAML (openapi.yaml) and is the single source of truth, with a rendered .pdf for distribution; the Risk Register and Traceability Matrix are maintained as spreadsheets and exported to .pdf for issue/sign-off snapshots.
- Diagrams are authored as source (Mermaid / Structurizr DSL / PlantUML) committed alongside the doc, then embedded as vector (SVG preferred) so they remain legible in black-and-white print.

3. PAGE GEOMETRY & TYPOGRAPHY (applies to all .docx / .pdf narrative docs)
- Page size A4 (210 x 297 mm), portrait. Tables/diagrams that need width may use landscape section breaks.
- Margins 2.5 cm on all four sides (top, bottom, left, right).
- Body text justified, single column, 11 pt serif (Cambria or Times New Roman) at 1.15 line spacing; 6 pt space after paragraphs. No first-line indent (block paragraphs).
- Headings: sans-serif (Calibri/Arial), bold, left-aligned, hierarchical numbering 1 / 1.1 / 1.1.1 (max depth 4). H1 14 pt, H2 12 pt, H3 11 pt; keep-with-next enabled; no heading orphaned at page bottom.
- Monospace (Consolas/Courier New, 10 pt) for code, identifiers, file paths, JSON, CLI commands, hashes, keys, and statute keys (e.g. "lawan-arus").
- Strictly black text on white; no color fills. Convey emphasis with bold/italic and grayscale shading (>= 15% contrast) only, never hue. Tables use 0.5 pt black rules with a light-gray (10-15%) header row.
- Lists: bulleted for unordered, decimal for sequential procedures; nested lists indented one level.

4. FRONT MATTER (every narrative document, in order)
- Title page: document title; project name "dashAI"; document type and Document ID; version and status (Draft | In Review | Approved | Superseded); author(s); document owner; classification (Public | Internal | Confidential — DPIA/Threat Model are Confidential); date; organization line. No logo unless supplied (black-and-white only).
- Document Control / Version History table: columns = Version | Date | Author | Description of Change | Reviewer/Approver. Append-only.
- Approval / Sign-off block: Name | Role | Signature | Date (DPIA and Project Plan require this; ADRs do not).
- Distribution list (Confidential docs).
- Automatic Table of Contents (field-generated from heading styles), followed by List of Figures and List of Tables (auto-generated from captions). Main-body page numbering (Arabic) starts after front matter; front matter uses lower-case roman numerals.

5. HEADERS, FOOTERS & IDENTIFICATION
- Header (every body page): document title (left) | classification marking (right).
- Footer (every body page): Document ID and version (left) | "Page X of Y" (right), where Y is the total page count.
- Document ID scheme: DASHAI-<TYPE>-<NNN>[-vMAJOR.MINOR]. Type codes: VS (Vision & Scope), PRD, SRS, SAD, ADR, DM (Data Model), API, TP (Test Plan), TM (Threat Model), DPIA, LCR (Legal Citation Reference), RUN (Deployment Runbook), UM (User Manual), PP (Project Plan), RR (Risk Register), RTM (Traceability Matrix). ADRs are sequentially numbered ADR-0001, ADR-0002, ... and are immutable/append-only (supersede, never edit).

6. CAPTIONS & CROSS-REFERENCES
- Figures captioned below: "Figure <chapter>-<n>: <description>" (e.g. "Figure 4-2: dashAI C4 Container diagram"). Tables captioned above: "Table <chapter>-<n>: <description>".
- All figures/tables are referenced by number in the body ("see Figure 4-2"), never by "below/above". Cross-document references use the Document ID. Requirement IDs (REQ-Fnn / REQ-NFRnn), ADR IDs, threat IDs (THR-nn), and risk IDs (RR-nn) are the canonical cross-cutting anchors that thread Vision -> PRD -> SRS -> SAD/ADR -> Test Plan -> RTM.

7. REQUIREMENTS & TRACEABILITY CONVENTIONS
- Every requirement is uniquely identified, atomic, verifiable, unambiguous, and traceable. Functional requirements use the ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 syntax: [condition] [subject] [action] [object] [constraint]. Numbering: REQ-Fnn (functional), REQ-NFRnn (non-functional/quality attribute).
- Each requirement carries a priority (MoSCoW: Must/Should/Could/Won't) and a verification method (Inspection/Analysis/Demonstration/Test). The RTM is bidirectional: forward (need -> requirement -> design/ADR -> module -> test) and backward (test -> requirement -> source); orphan requirements (untested) and orphan tests (scope creep) are flagged.

8. STANDARDS ANCHORS (cite the exact edition; do not cite superseded standards as current)
- SRS: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 (NOT IEEE 830). Architecture: ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010:2022 + C4 model (C4 is pragmatic views layered inside a 42010-conforming description; 42010 still requires stakeholders, concerns, viewpoints, views, decisions, and correspondences). ADR: Michael Nygard format (MADR for option-heavy decisions); immutable/sequential. Test docs: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-3:2021 (NOT IEEE 829). API: OpenAPI 3.1.x (full JSON Schema 2020-12; at least one of paths/components/webhooks present). Threat model: STRIDE with explicit trust boundaries + per-threat mitigation/owner/residual risk (OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet). DPIA: GDPR Art. 35 + ICO 7-step; assess risks to data subjects' rights & freedoms (not security only), completed BEFORE high-risk processing, with DPO advice + residual-risk sign-off. User docs: ISO/IEC/IEEE 26514:2022. Vision & Scope: Karl Wiegers template. Project plan + RACI: PMBOK (each task >= 1 Responsible, exactly 1 Accountable).

9. BACK MATTER (every narrative document, as applicable)
- Glossary (domain + crypto + Indonesian-legal terms); List of Acronyms/Abbreviations (e.g. LLAJ, OSM, CV, OCR, PII, DPIA, STRIDE, ADR, RTM, SLO); References/Bibliography (numbered, with the exact standard editions and URLs); Appendices.

10. QUALITY BAR & CONSISTENCY
- One concept, one term, used identically across the whole suite. Use a single template/stylesheet so heading styles, captions, and tables are uniform. Keep all documents living and version-controlled; update the Document Control table on every change. Distinguish layers rigorously: Vision & Scope = why/boundaries (business), PRD = what for users/market (product), SRS = engineering-precise verifiable requirements. Never label one as another.

Feature-to-release roadmap (initial vs subsequent, by detection tier)

1. Overview / Summary
2. Problem statement & background (citizen-captured traffic violation evidence; trust and privacy problem)
3. Goals and Non-goals (explicit out-of-scope: real-time fines, server-side video storage, identifying individuals beyond plate)
4. Success Metrics / KPIs & Release Criteria
